incorrects.description: Student selected 'Count everything': doesn't recognize the need to combine before comparing

params.description: Second scenario highlights: Which team scored more—the Red team alone, or the Blue and Green teams combined?

steps.text: Look at the second question. Red team is alone. Blue and Green need to be put together first. What do we do before we compare?

incorrects.description: Student selected 'Find the biggest number': focused on individual values rather than the combining pattern

params.description: First scenario highlights: Who has more stickers—just Maya, or Ben and Carlos put together?

steps.text: The first question compares Maya alone to Ben and Carlos put together. We can't just find the biggest number. What do we do with Ben and Carlos first?

incorrects.description: Student selected 'Subtract twice': confused operation with the structure of combining then comparing

params.description: Third scenario highlights: Did I spend more on only snacks, or on drinks and dessert altogether?

steps.text: Look at the third question. Drinks and dessert need to be put together. That's adding, not subtracting. Then we compare that total to snacks.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: All three scenarios highlight the shared pattern: one thing alone versus two things combined

steps.text: Let me show you the pattern. In every question, one side stands alone and the other side has two things that need combining. You combine first, then you compare the two sides. The answer is combine some things first, then compare.
